title: Unveiling the Interplay of Charge and Magnetic Excitations in HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+δ}$
authors: Karn Rongrueangkul, Martina Fedele, Leonardo Martinelli, Giacomo Merzoni, Roberto Sant, Nicholas B. Brookes, Dorothée Colson, Alain Sacuto, Götz Seibold, Sergio Caprara, Marco Moretti Sala, Giacomo Ghiringhelli, Riccardo Arpaia
abstract: Unraveling the mechanism that binds electrons into Cooper pairs in cuprate high-temperature superconductors remains one of the most fundamental challenges in condensed-matter physics. While both magnetic interactions and lattice vibrations are known to govern key electronic properties, their possible cooperation has never been directly observed. We investigate HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+δ}$ (Hg1223) - the cuprate with the highest $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ at ambient pressure - as a magnifying glass to probe the possible entwining of the excitations at the core of the pairing. Using resonant inelastic X-ray scattering, we find that the charge response is dominated by dynamic charge density fluctuations (CDF) extending up to several hundred meV, where magnetic excitations reside. At the same momentum where CDF are most intense, the paramagnon energy exhibits a pronounced softening, revealing a strong interplay among charge, lattice, and spin excitations. Our results point to a cooperative mechanism in which dynamic charge fluctuations mediate the coupling between lattice, charge and spin degrees of freedom-shedding new light on the fundamental origin of high-$T_{\mathrm{c}}$ superconductivity.
summary: This research uses RIXS spectroscopy on high-Tc cuprates to investigate the coupling between charge, spin, and lattice excitations. The key finding is that dynamic charge fluctuations mediate this interplay, causing paramagnon softening without implying a competing static CDW order. This suggests a cooperative mechanism underlying high-temperature superconductivity.
main_problem: The central challenge is elucidating the mechanism responsible for forming Cooper pairs (high-Tc superconductivity) in cuprates, specifically by investigating the cooperative interplay between charge, lattice, and spin excitations.
main_result: Dynamic Charge Density Fluctuations (CDF), extending to high energies, mediate a strong coupling between charge and spin degrees of freedom. This coupling appears to promote superconductivity rather than competing with it, unlike static CDW order.
method: The study combines advanced experimental techniques like Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS) with sophisticated theoretical modeling using Green's functions and phenomenological self-energy calculations.
model_or_system: The primary system is the cuprate superconductor HgBa2Ca2Cu3O(8+delta) (Hg1223). The analysis involves fitting RIXS spectra to extract spectral weights for dynamic charge fluctuations, phonon anomalies, and paramagnon dispersions using theoretical models incorporating electron-phonon coupling.
key_observables: Dynamic Charge Density Fluctuations (CDF), Paramagnon energy/dispersion ($\omega(\mathbf{k})$), Phonon softening anomaly, and the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$).
important_parameters: The strength of the coupling is parameterized by $\Gamma$ (in theoretical models) or observed through spectral weight changes; key momentum points include $q_{	ext{CDF}} = 0.30 	ext{ r.l.u.}$ along $(	ext{H}, 0)$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the high-energy tail of CDF is crucial for coupling to spin excitations, and that phonon softening observed at $q_{	ext{CDF}}$ is due to these dynamic fluctuations rather than static CDW formation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate RIXS spectra showing distinct contributions from elastic scattering (CDW/CDF), phonons, and paramagnons across various temperatures and crystal directions, demonstrating the energy overlap between CDF and magnetic excitations.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the experimental observation of coupled fluctuations in Hg1223 using RIXS. It then develops theoretical models (using self-energy corrections) to quantify how dynamic charge fluctuations mediate the observed paramagnon softening, contrasting this with static CDW scenarios.
why_interesting: The work provides a concrete mechanism—dynamical charge mediation—that links lattice vibrations, electronic correlations, and magnetic order in a highly complex quantum material, offering insights into emergent collective behavior relevant to strongly correlated physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Crystal structure, phase diagram, and experimental conditions of HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+δ (Hg1223). (a) Crystal structure of Hg1223. (b) Superconducting dome of Hg1223, with Tc values from samples both as those of our study and from the literature. Their dependence follows a parabolic relationship, without any hint of anomalous Tc suppres- sion in the underdoped region. RIXS spectra were acquired in medium resolution mode at several temperatures between 20 and 300 K (yellow bar), and in high resolution mode at 110 K, i.e., just below the superconducting transition, and at room temperature (stars). (c) High- resolution (∆E = 32 meV) RIXS maps measured at T = 110 K along the (H, 0) and (H,...

Figure 2: Charge, lattice, and magnetic excitations in Hg1223 from RIXS. From the fits as a function of momentum along the (H, 0) direction we extract: (a) the area of the elastic line, (b) the area of the CDF peak, (c) the dispersion and (d) the area of the bond- stretching phonons. (e) Inelastic RIXS maps along the (H, 0) and (H, H) directions obtained by subtracting from the raw spectra all low-energy contributions identified in the fits of panels (a–d), so as to single out the spectral weight associated with the paramagnons. (f) Disper- sion of the paramagnon energy ωmax, fitted using both the nearest-neighbour Heisenberg model (dashed line) and a phenomenological linear spin-wave model...

Figure 3: Interplay of paramagnon softening and charge-density fluctuations. (a) The CDF peak at different temperatures between 20 and 300 K, highlighted by the RIXS inten- sity integrated in the range [–0.1, 0.035] eV. (b,c) Height and FWHM of the Lorentzian profiles used to fit the CDF peak. Both intensity and correlation length increase upon cooling and satu- rate below Tc. (d–g) Paramagnon energy ωmax extracted from the fits at various temperatures, compared with the phenomenological linear spin-wave model including four nearest-neighbour exchange parameters. (h) Paramagnon softening at each temperature, quantified by the de- viation between experimental data and the fitted spin-wave...

Figure 4: Broad energy range of charge-density fluctuations overlapping with para- magnons. (a) To assess the energy extent of the CDF peak, each RIXS spectrum was divided into several energy intervals, and the integrated intensity was extracted for each of them. (b–e) Example of this procedure at T = 110 K and 300 K, using 60 meV–wide adjacent intervals cov- ering the range from –30 to 210 meV. While the CDF peak is maximal in the quasi-elastic region [–30, 30] meV, at low temperature it survives up to 210 meV, whereas at room temperature this high-energy component vanishes. (f) To better visualize the evolution of the CDF peak with energy, at T = 110 K the analysis was repeated with 60...

Figure 5: Phenomenological model for paramagnon softening induced by charge- density fluctuations. Renormalized paramagnon dispersion eωk obtained from the phenomeno- logical self-energy describing the coupling between paramagnons and CDF. The bare dispersion ωk (Γ →∞, blue line) evolves towards the reconstructed dispersion ω′ k (Γ →0, grey line) through an intermediate regime (Γ = 2, violet line), where a pronounced softening develops at qCDF. The parameter Γ captures the dynamical character of the CDF and effectively encodes the strength of the electron–phonon coupling: smaller Γ corresponds to slower, more correlated fluctuations and leads to stronger softening.
